title: First Steps on Mercury
narratorText: We have touched down on Mercury for the first time. The surface is alien — ancient, scarred, and brutally hot — but it is ours to begin understanding. The real work starts now.

title: The First Days
narratorText: We have been on Mercury long enough now that the crew has stopped calling it an expedition and started calling it home. That shift — small as it sounds — changes everything about how we plan.

title: The Fire We Have Always Wanted
narratorText: We have cracked the last theoretical barrier. Deuterium fusion is no longer a dream on a whiteboard — it is a blueprint waiting for the infrastructure to give it form. The energy age is almost here.

title: We Are Type I
narratorText: We have learned to draw on our star at a planetary scale, and the future now feels like something we can build rather than merely survive. The Dyson array grows daily. Our ambitions grow faster.

title: The First Era
narratorText: We are no longer a species clinging to one wounded cradle. We have made multiple worlds habitable and kept going. The first era of interplanetary civilisation is complete. What comes next is unwritten.

title: We Are Type II
narratorText: We have wrapped our industry around the Sun so completely that the scale of our work is no longer planetary — it is stellar. The Dyson sphere is complete. We are the light-weavers now.

title: Into the Dark Between Stars
narratorText: We have not become a galactic civilisation — not yet. But we have sent a living promise beyond the Sun and into the dark between stars. Whatever finds it one day will know we were here.

title: Ten Percent
narratorText: One in ten of the Sun's rays now strikes a panel we built. From here, the growth compounds. We can feel the energy budget of the solar system beginning to tip in our favour.

title: A Quarter of the Sun
narratorText: A quarter of the Sun's output now passes through our hands before it reaches the inner planets. This is not just energy. This is control of a star.

title: Halfway to the Stars
narratorText: We have passed the halfway point. The Sun is half-wrapped in our ambition. Fifty years ago this would have been mythology. Now it is an infrastructure report.

title: The First Ocean
narratorText: We have confirmed it. Standing water — persistent, open, real — on the surface of Mars. Every biologist we have is weeping. We understand why.

title: The First Birthday
narratorText: Someone on the lunar base turned thirty today — for the first time in their life, away from Earth. There was a cake. They used powdered ingredients and a microwave-adapted heating element. It was, by most accounts, terrible. Everyone ate two slices.

title: Those Who Will Not Come Home
narratorText: Three crew members have formally requested to remain on the lunar base permanently. They are healthy, productive, and clear-eyed about what they are choosing. They say Earth no longer feels like home. Do we honour that?

choices.label: Honour their choice. They know what they are.
Recall them. The mission needs rotation discipline.

title: The Great Silence
narratorText: Our long-range signal arrays have mapped a significant portion of nearby stellar neighbourhood. Nothing. Not a flicker of intentional radio, not a structured pulse, nothing that looks built. We are alone — or the silence is a warning. What do we do with this?

choices.label: Broadcast our existence. Let anyone who is listening know we are here.
Go quiet. The silence may be a survival strategy.
Continue as normal. The silence changes nothing.

title: The Mirror Goes Up
narratorText: The first orbital shade mirror is in position. Venus, for the first time in four billion years, is receiving less sunlight than yesterday. We are turning down the heat of a planet. The atmosphere will take centuries to respond. We are patient.

title: The Long Explosion
narratorText: We have detonated the polar ice cap charges. The shockwave is visible from orbit. The CO2 sublimation curve will run for decades. It is not subtle — but it is fast. The naturalists are already drafting their objections. They are not wrong. We did it anyway.

title: Something Stirs in the Ice
narratorText: Deep-range sonar from the Europa monitoring stations has detected structured pressure waves in the sub-ice ocean. They are not geological. We do not know what they are. We have decided, for now, not to broadcast this.

title: We Redirected Europa
narratorText: The vote passed by eleven. We have fired the mass drivers and committed Europa to a Venus intercept trajectory. Whatever was in the ocean is now our responsibility. Some things cannot be undone. We voted for this one anyway.
